senses.gloss: to be near, to come near, to approach
to be related, to be of close relations, to be kindred (especially on the maternal side)
to have relation to something, to be associated
to be engaged with, to be or become involved with
to be near, to come near, to approach

examples.english: Do not approach the lion lest he eat you.

examples.text: وَقُلْنَا يَا آدَمُ ٱسْكُنْ أَنْتَ وَزَوْجُكَ الْجَنَّةَ وَكُلَا مِنْهَا رَغَدًا حَيْثُ شِئْتُمَا وَلَا تَقْرَبَا هَٰذِهِ ٱلشَّجَرَةَ فَتَكُونَا مِنَ ٱلظَّالِمِين

senses.gloss: to cause or allow to be near

examples.text: عِنْدَمَا كَانَ جَالِسًا بِالْقُرْبِ مِنْ زَوْجَتِهِ... صَرَخَتْ فِي وَجْهِهِ قَائِلَةً: ”اِبْتَعِدْ عَنِّي!“
examples.english: When he was sitting next to his wife... she yelled into his face, saying: “Go away from me!”

examples.text:
أنا ليه بحب حبك قوي كده ليه
 وأموت في قربك أنا كده ليه
examples.english:
ʾana leih bi-ḥubbi ḥubbak qawī kida leih
 wa-'mūtu fī qorbak ʾana kida leih

etymology.text:
* From the root ق ر ب (q r b), with the form I being reconstructed securely as Proto-Semitic *ḳarib-, likewise the form II as Proto-Semitic *ḳarrib-.
* While the Biblical Hebrew קָרַב (qāráḇ, “to approach”) governs prepositions of approach like אֶל (el), לְ (lə), עַל (ʿal), the Arabic قَرُبَ (qaruba) has obtained the opposited preposition مِن (min) by analogy of بَعُدَ (baʕuda, “to be far”); compare the same with دَنَا (danā, “to come near”).
